---
title: "Interactive Visualizations"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
---

This page contains three interactive visualizations built from the final project dataset, satisfying the Homework 5 requirement.

## Figure 1: Delay Trend with Precipitation

This is a daily time-series of TTC total delay minutes (full calendar days), with a 30-day moving average and daily precipitation overlay. The plot distinguishes isolated spikes from more persistent weather-related delay patterns while preserving the source-coverage structure of the panel. By tracking the blue daily series first, then the orange rolling trend, and finally comparing both against the teal precipitation bars on the secondary axis, you can read short-term shocks and medium-term shifts together.

<iframe src="outputs/viz1_timeseries.html" width="100%" height="560" style="border:2px solid #b7cae0; border-radius:6px;"></iframe>

**Caption.** Daily TTC delay minutes over time with a 30-day moving average and precipitation overlay.

## Figure 2: Monthly Mean Daily Delay (Year x Month)

This is a year-by-month heatmap of mean daily TTC delay minutes. The plot highlights longer-run seasonal structure and multi-year shifts that are difficult to see in the raw day-level series. By scanning darker cells first and then comparing patterns across rows (year effects) and columns (seasonal effects), you can quickly identify when delay intensity was persistently elevated.

<iframe src="outputs/viz2_heatmap.html" width="100%" height="560" style="border:2px solid #b7cae0; border-radius:6px;"></iframe>

**Caption.** Monthly mean daily delay minutes arranged by year and month.

## Figure 3: Weekday vs Weekend Delay by Weather Type

This is a weekday-versus-weekend boxplot comparison within each weather category, estimated on incident-recorded days. The plot is useful because it separates day-type effects from weather-category effects without conflating missing-record days with true zero-delay outcomes. By comparing medians and interquartile ranges between weekday and weekend boxes inside each weather group, you can assess both average shift and variability differences.

<iframe src="outputs/viz3_boxplot.html" width="100%" height="560" style="border:2px solid #b7cae0; border-radius:6px;"></iframe>

**Caption.** Boxplot comparison of daily delay minutes across weather categories by weekday/weekend (incident days only).
